label: SES-026 v0.5 Conversation 1 closeout: 9 decisions, 1 session, 1 planning item, 9 references

session.title: v0.5 Conversation 1 — multi-engagement architecture and engagement schema
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation on 05-16-26 that opened against the seed prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-1-kickoff.md. Combined multi-engagement architecture design with engagement methodology-entity schema design because the two were tightly coupled (engagement record field shape depends on routing decisions). Settled the ten architectural questions catalogued in the v0.5 workstream plan §6, produced two deliverable documents at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-engagement-architecture.md and PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md, and queued v0.5 Conversation 2 (build planning) as the next conversation in the workstream. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-conversation-1-kickoff.md against the project default ARCHITECTURE operating mode.

Pre-flight. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md (the ten architectural questions agenda), methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, methodology-schema-specs/domain.md end-to-end plus the §3 heading structure of entity.md, process.md, and crm_candidate.md (confirming template uniformity and noting process's no-status-field deviation and crm_candidate's removed-status deviation), the v0.4 PRD §2/§6/§7-9, the v2 runtime config.py (existing env-var override mechanism), v1's automation/db/master_schema.py (Client.code regex constraint and master-DB pattern precedent), v1's automation/ui/active_client_context.py (in-memory + signal + dual-persistence pattern), DEC-039 (the original multi-tenancy finding), and the SES-025 close-out payload (DEC-075/076/077). Verified SES-025 close-out had been applied: sessions=25, decisions=77, three decided_in refs to SES-025 present, PI-001 status still Open with description amended. git status clean; git pull --rebase clean.

Framing note before Q1. DEC-039 is the prior context being operationalised, not superseded. The weak priors in the kickoff all match DEC-039's framing exactly. Two precedents banked for later questions: v1's database_path column was deprecated in favor of convention (real-world supporting evidence for Q2's convention-based-paths posture); v1's active-state persistence is actually hybrid (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences), which informed Q3's framing.

Q1 — Engagement discovery model. Bootstrap meta DB vs directory scan. Option A approved: bootstrap meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db with an engagements registry table. Separates routing metadata from methodology content; mirrors v1's master-DB pattern; supports operations on inactive engagements. Sub-question resolved later in the conversation: the engagement record IS the meta DB row (not a pointer to records inside per-engagement DBs).

Q2 — Per-engagement DB file location. Engine-repo conventional vs split vs convention-with-override. Option A approved: engine repo conventional paths at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db, gitignored. No path field on engagement record. v1's split is a real precedent but adopting it now adds coupling to client-repo structure that v0.5's known use cases don't require.

Q7 — Dogfood migration. (Taken next because Q1 + Q2 settled enough foundation to make Q7's options concrete.) One-shot explicit migration vs permanent dual-path vs configurable path. Option A approved: one-shot explicit migration with backup-first, verify-row-counts, then delete-original discipline. Idempotent on rerun. Three install scenarios handled: existing v2 install (migrate), fresh install (no-op, meta DB empty, user creates engagements explicitly), rerun after successful migration (exit cleanly). The 24 sessions / 74 decisions / 16 PIs / 66 references / 222 change_log entries / 42 catalog entries / 13 status versions / 2 charter versions all survive.

Q3 — Active-engagement state persistence. Two distinct facts framed: live state (singleton at v2-install level) and ordering (per-engagement). Option A approved for live state: JSON file at crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json holding {engagement_identifier, engagement_code, set_at} plus in-memory ActiveEngagementContext QObject mirroring v1's pattern. Ordering: engagements.last_opened_at column on the meta DB. The hybrid mirrors v1's actual pattern.

Q4 — API server model. One-process-per-engagement vs multi-tenant vs hot-swap admin endpoint. Option A approved for v0.5 (one process per engagement, kill-and-relaunch on switch) with the user-added direction that Option B (multi-tenant) be committed as a follow-on migration. Migration timing clarified through a brief back-and-forth: not 'v0.6 specifically' or 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate' but 'when v2 transitions from prototype to production.' This trigger is a planned transition rather than a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern). PI-017 authored to track the migration work.

Q9 — MCP server routing. Forced by Q4. Decided-and-announced: MCP server lifecycle mirrors API — one MCP subprocess per engagement, killed and relaunched on switch. Same constraints, same migration path to multi-tenant at prototype-to-production transition.

Q5 — Identifier scope. Decided-and-announced: per-engagement scope for governance and methodology identifiers (SES, DEC, PI, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC) — happens automatically because v0.4 next-identifier helpers query the active DB connection. Engagement identifiers (ENG) scope to the meta DB — one sequence per v2 install.

Q6 — Migrations across engagements. Lazy at engagement-open vs eager at v2 launch vs explicit only. Option A approved: lazy at engagement-open, mirroring v1's run_client_migrations pattern. Defers work to when needed; failure modes scoped to one engagement at a time; never-opened engagements pay no cost. Meta DB has its own Alembic chain that applies at desktop launch.

Q8 — Per-engagement exports. Split (dogfood in engine repo, client in client repo) via engagement_export_dir field vs all in engine repo vs manual command only. Option A approved: nullable engagement_export_dir absolute-path column on the meta DB; dogfood's value set by the v0.5 migration to the computed engine-repo path; client engagement values set at creation time via the New-Engagement dialog. Null disables auto-export. Q1 sub-question resolved here as a downstream implication: engagement record lives in the meta DB row.

Q10b — Engagement status lifecycle. active/paused/archived with free transitions vs domain pattern (candidate/confirmed/deferred) vs two-state (active/archived). Option A approved: active/paused/archived with free transitions, default starter active. Engagement records are direct creations (not propose-verified by a client), so domain's one-way gate doesn't fit. Three states capture real operational lifecycle without ceremony; free transitions match reality (engagements can resume from any state). Soft-delete independent of status.

Q10 — Engagement entity field set (strawman ratification). Ten fields approved: engagement_identifier (ENG-NNN, server-assigned), engagement_code (regex ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$ mirroring v1's Client.code constraint exactly, case-insensitive unique), engagement_name (required, case-insensitive unique), engagement_purpose (required, plain text 1-2 sentences), engagement_status (enum active/paused/archived, default active, free transitions), engagement_last_opened_at (nullable, for ordering), engagement_export_dir (nullable absolute path, validated when set), engagement_created_at / engagement_updated_at / engagement_deleted_at (inherited base behavior). All prefixed engagement_ per DEC-046. Three deliberate omissions from the domain pattern: no engagement_description (purpose suffices), no engagement_notes (engagement is routing metadata not methodology content; scratchpad notes belong in the per-engagement DB), no engagement_db_path (Q2 chose convention). No relationships in v0.5 (engagement stands alone in the meta DB; per-engagement DB entities don't reference engagement records because engagement is implicit context for everything in the engagement's own DB). Identifier prefix ENG (3 letters, no collision with existing prefixes). Standard endpoint set served from the meta DB (not from the active engagement's DB). No activate endpoint — activation is desktop-side orchestration (kill API → write current_engagement.json → PATCH last_opened_at → launch new API → emit signal). UI deviation justified: engagement is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata, not domain-scope content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group; two needed affordances (switching UI and management UI); exact visual shape deferred to Conversation 2 in coordination with the styling workstream. Dogfood engagement record (CRMBUILDER) field values defined for migration time.

Architectural detail surfaced and recorded. The v0.5 API server connects to two SQLite databases simultaneously: the meta DB (for /engagements/* endpoints) and the active engagement's DB (for everything else). Recorded in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10 and engagement.md §3.5.1.

Activation sequence specified. 12-step desktop-side orchestration documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4: reachability check, pre-flight Alembic migration, kill API+MCP subprocesses, write current_engagement.json, PATCH engagement_last_opened_at, update in-memory context, launch new API+MCP subprocesses, emit signal. Total elapsed time: a few seconds for a typical switch.

Deliverables produced and committed. Two documents drafted in-conversation: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-engagement-architecture.md (system-level architecture document) and PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md (engagement schema spec conforming to methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md). Close-out payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_026.json. Apply prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-026.md.

Close-out path chosen. Path 1 (Claude Code apply prompt, mirroring the SES-025 precedent) — the apply prompt POSTs the standard payload via apply_close_out.py.
session.artifacts_produced: Two deliverable documents committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/: multi-engagement-architecture.md (system-level architecture covering the ten settled questions plus the two-database API server detail plus the activation sequence) and methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md (engagement entity schema spec conforming to methodology-entity-schema-spec-guide.md template; 12 acceptance criteria; UI-shape deviation justified). Plus the close-out payload PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_026.json (this payload) and the apply prompt PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-026.md. All four artifacts committed to origin/main in one commit by Claude per the in-sandbox close-out convention.
session.in_flight_at_end: v0.5 Conversation 2 (build planning) is the next conversation in the workstream. Kickoff document to be authored by Doug (or by a small wrap-up step in this conversation if Doug requests it). Conversation 2 takes multi-engagement-architecture.md and engagement.md as inputs; produces ui-PRD-v0.5.md, ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md, and the slice build prompts. The PI-001 styling workstream (parallel per DEC-076) is independent and runs against its own kickoff. After Conversation 2 closes, build slice execution begins via Claude Code. Status remains 'v0.4 complete' until v0.5 actually ships. The paper-test conversation (DEC-077) stays deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created.

decisions.title: Engagement discovery model: bootstrap meta DB at fixed path with engagements registry table
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's first architectural question (Q1 in the workstream plan §6). v2 needs a mechanism to know what engagements exist on this machine at startup, populate the picker UI, and decide which engagement is active. Two viable options framed: (A) a small bootstrap meta SQLite file at a fixed path with an engagements registry table; (B) directory scan of per-engagement DB files in a known directory. Coupled to the engagement record's location (does it live in the meta DB or inside each per-engagement DB?), to the meaning of operations on inactive engagements, and to drift-recovery posture.
decisions.decision: Bootstrap meta DB at the fixed path crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. The meta DB has its own Alembic migration chain (separate from per-engagement chains) and is gitignored. Creating an engagement performs two writes: INSERT into the meta DB's engagements table and create the corresponding per-engagement DB file. Discovery is a SELECT against the meta DB. Engagement endpoints (GET /engagements, POST /engagements, etc.) read and write the meta DB exclusively. The engagement record IS the meta DB row (not a lightweight pointer to records inside each per-engagement DB) — this sub-question is resolved here as well, as a consequence of the meta DB being the canonical home for engagement routing metadata.
decisions.rationale: Separates routing metadata ('what engagements exist on this machine') from methodology content ('what's inside an engagement'). Mirrors v1's master-DB pattern (automation/db/master_schema.py), which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. Carries metadata (status, last-opened-at, purpose, export_dir) outside any single engagement's DB so engagement records can be queried, renamed, or archived without opening each per-engagement DB. The 'engagement record inside its own per-engagement DB' alternative is self-referential and complicates the discovery semantics; the meta DB framing is cleaner. The additional SQLite file is a real cost but small; the drift mode (per-engagement DB file moved or deleted out-of-band) is handled by a reachability check at activation time, mirroring v1's automation/core/client_reachability.py pattern.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — directory scan of crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/*.db with engagement metadata stored inside each engagement's own DB. Rejected because populating the picker requires opening every DB, and engagement metadata (status, last_opened_at, purpose, export_dir) is routing-level concern that doesn't naturally belong inside per-engagement methodology content. Renaming / soft-deleting / archiving operations on inactive engagements would either require opening each DB or living with a degraded picker. Option C (hybrid: scan the filesystem AND maintain a meta DB cache) was considered briefly and rejected as needlessly complex — the meta DB alone is sufficient.
decisions.consequences: v0.5 ships a meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db (gitignored) with its own Alembic chain. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) creates the meta DB and inserts the CRMBUILDER engagement row. The engagement record lives as a row in the meta DB; the engagement entity schema (DEC-086) follows this placement. The meta DB drift mode is handled by reachability checks; if a per-engagement DB file is missing at activation time, activation fails with a UX message and the user is prompted via the picker. The meta DB's own migrations apply at desktop launch (failure to migrate is a hard-fail UX). Future enhancement (v0.6+, candidate): a small reconciliation tool that detects out-of-band file moves and offers to update the meta DB's engagement records accordingly; not in v0.5 scope.

decisions.title: Per-engagement DB file location: engine repo with conventional paths derived from engagement_code
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q2. Each engagement's SQLite file needs a location on disk. Three viable options: (A) all per-engagement DB files in the engine repo at a conventional path, (B) split — engine DB in engine repo, client engagement DBs in client repos at {project_folder}/.crmbuilder-v2/{code}.db (mirroring v1's split), (C) convention with optional override field on the engagement record. Coupled to Q8 (exports location) and to whether the engagement record carries a path field. v1 has been doing the split (Option B's equivalent) for two years and it works; v1 originally had a database_path column on Client but deprecated it in favor of convention {project_folder}/.crmbuilder/{code}.db — real-world supporting evidence that convention wins over explicit field at this granularity.
decisions.decision: All per-engagement DB files live at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db in the engine repo, gitignored. The engagement record carries no path field in v0.5. Dogfood DB after migration is crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db; CBM's will be crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CBM.db. Filename derived from engagement_code (uppercase per the engagement_code constraint).
decisions.rationale: Minimum viable for v0.5; handles both dogfood and CBM cleanly. v1's split is a real precedent worth respecting, but adopting it now adds an engagement-record path field plus a coupling to client-repo structure that v0.5's known use cases (Doug on one laptop, dogfood + CBM active) don't justify. Future migration to v1-style split is small (add nullable engagement_db_path column; null means convention, set means override), so deferring the choice doesn't block it. v1's own deprecation of its database_path column in favor of convention is real-world supporting evidence that the path-field flexibility was not actually used at this granularity.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — split (engine DB in engine repo, client DBs in client repos via required engagement_db_path field). Rejected because it requires adding a path field to the engagement record (per Q10) and couples v0.5 to client-repo structure conventions that v0.5's use cases don't actually require. The portability win (client repo cloned to new machine 'comes with' engagement state) is real but theoretical for v0.5's known use cases; deferring it is fine. Option C — convention with nullable override field. Rejected as a compromise that defers a real decision: the field either exists or it doesn't; 'nullable optional override' usually evolves into 'we should have made it explicit one way or the other.' Q2 commits to no field in v0.5; v0.6+ migration adds the field if and when the use case justifies it.
decisions.consequences: .gitignore gains crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/. Engagement record schema (DEC-086) does not include engagement_db_path. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) moves crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. When (if) the engagement-DB-travels-with-client-repo use case surfaces, a v0.6+ migration adds nullable engagement_db_path column to the meta DB engagements table; existing rows stay null and continue using convention; new rows or edited rows may set the override. Acknowledged asymmetry with Q8 (exports do split between engine and client repos via a path field) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.

decisions.title: Active-engagement state persistence: JSON file for live state plus engagements.last_opened_at column for ordering plus in-memory ActiveEngagementContext
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q3. v2 needs a mechanism for the desktop, API subprocess, MCP subprocess, and external scripts to know which engagement is currently active, plus a mechanism for the picker to order engagements by recency. Conversation surfaced that two distinct facts are being persisted: live state ('what is the engine pointing at right now', a singleton at the v2-install level, used by API/MCP at startup, by external scripts that want to know who they're talking to, and by the desktop to restore last-active on launch) and ordering ('when was each engagement last opened', per-engagement metadata, used by the picker). Splitting them clarified the framing — the original Q3 framing conflated the two. v1's active-state persistence is itself a hybrid (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences).
decisions.decision: Two-channel persistence with each channel canonical for one fact. (1) Live state: a small JSON file at crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json holds {engagement_identifier, engagement_code, set_at}, atomically written by the desktop on engagement activation. Plus an in-memory ActiveEngagementContext QObject with an active_engagement_changed(object) Qt signal, mirroring v1's ActiveClientContext shape. The desktop reads the file on launch to restore the last-active engagement. (2) Ordering: a last_opened_at DATETIME column on the meta DB engagements table (specifically engagement_last_opened_at per the parent-prefix field-naming convention), updated by the desktop at activation. Used by the picker to surface recent engagements first.
decisions.rationale: Mirrors v1's hybrid pattern (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences) which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. The JSON file affords external peek/curl access without an extra API endpoint or SQL helper. The meta-DB column affords queryable ordering for the picker without the API needing to read the JSON file. Separation of concerns matches the underlying reality: ordering is per-engagement metadata; live pointer is a singleton at the v2-install level. The original Q3 framing's three options (file on disk, column in meta DB, env var only) all conflated the two facts; splitting them clarifies that the question is really about the live state, and the meta-DB column is useful regardless.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — singleton row in the meta DB (a one-row active_engagement table or an is_current boolean column on engagements). Rejected because external clients (curl, MCP tools, diagnostic scripts) cannot peek without an API call or SQL helper, and the file/DB drift risk is low in practice because the desktop is the only writer to both. Option C — environment variable only, no persistence. Rejected because it loses the 'open the app, last engagement is loaded' UX without producing meaningful simplification. The hybrid framing (split the two facts) was the framing change that resolved the conversation; once the two facts were named, the file-for-live + column-for-ordering answer was uncontroversial.
decisions.consequences: crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json added to .gitignore. engagement_last_opened_at column added to the meta DB engagements table (DATETIME, nullable). Activation sequence in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 includes both writes (file and column). Drift recovery on app launch: if current_engagement.json references an engagement that doesn't exist in the meta DB or whose DB file is missing, the context starts empty and the picker UX prompts for selection (no silent fallback). The in-memory ActiveEngagementContext follows v1's ActiveClientContext pattern (QObject, Qt signal, reachability check on activation, signal emission). External scripts can cat the file to know which engagement is currently active.

decisions.title: v0.5 API and MCP server model: one process per engagement bound at spawn; commitment to multi-tenant migration at prototype-to-production transition
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q4 and Q9 (Q9 forced by Q4). The API server today (v0.4) is a subprocess spawned by the desktop at startup, reading CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH from env at startup and serving one DB on port 8765. v0.5 needs to support multiple engagements. Two main viable options: (A) one API subprocess per engagement, bound at spawn, killed and relaunched on engagement switch; (B) multi-tenant single process serving all engagements via header or path-prefix routing. Option D (hot-swap admin endpoint) was named but rejected for mutable-cross-request-state reasons. Q9 (MCP) follows whichever Q4 settles. User directed: approve A for v0.5 but add a decision committing to B. Migration timing was clarified through a brief back-and-forth: not 'v0.6 specifically' or 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate' but 'when v2 transitions from prototype to production.'
decisions.decision: v0.5 ships one API subprocess per engagement (Option A): the API subprocess is spawned by the desktop launcher with CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH set to the active engagement's DB path, serves on port 8765 for that engagement's lifetime, and is killed and relaunched on engagement switch (the desktop's kill-relaunch dance). The API server's existing endpoint code is unchanged from v0.4 — no concept of engagement, no request-routing layer. The MCP server has the same lifecycle (one MCP subprocess per engagement, mirroring the API). Committed for the longer term: multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture. The migration from one-process-per-engagement to multi-tenant is committed; it lands as part of the production-readiness workstream when v2 transitions from prototype to production. This anchors PI-017.
decisions.rationale: For v0.5: scope is 'make multiple engagements possible,' not 'redesign the API layer.' Option A delivers the former without doing the latter. The API server code stays as-shipped in v0.4; engagement-routing complexity lives in the desktop's launcher and switcher logic, where new code is going anyway. Consistent with DEC-039's framing ('one v2 instance per engagement, separate SQLite, separate API port'). For the longer term: kill-relaunch is intentional for the v0.x prototype phase but inappropriate for production. Cross-engagement queries, dashboards, mid-flight switching all become desirable as v2 stabilises. Committing to the migration at the prototype-to-production transition avoids the PI-001 anti-pattern (friction triggers that never fire) while keeping the v0.5 scope bounded. The trigger is a planned transition rather than friction signal.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B for v0.5 — multi-tenant from the start. Rejected for v0.5 because it requires refactoring every endpoint to accept engagement context (header or path-prefix), refactoring the access-layer connection model, refactoring tests, and reshaping the MCP server — substantial scope addition that v0.5's stated scope did not include. Option D — hot-swap admin endpoint (single API process with POST /admin/switch-engagement). Rejected because it introduces mutable cross-request state that complicates testing and creates mid-request switch race risk; convenience of fast switching isn't worth the testability cost. Option C — dynamic ports per engagement (multiple subprocesses serving simultaneously on different ports). Rejected because v0.5's use case is one engagement active at a time; the port-management complexity isn't justified. For the migration trigger: 'v0.6 specifically' was named but rejected because forcing CBM Phase 1 to wait for the migration is unnecessary scope tightening; 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate before features' was named but rejected because it under-specifies the trigger; the prototype-to-production framing is what Doug clarified and what fits the v2 charter narrative.
decisions.consequences: v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements the kill-relaunch dance in the desktop's switch path (Slice C in the strawman). The API server itself requires no endpoint refactor for v0.5 — only the addition of meta-DB connection handling for /engagements/* endpoints (per DEC-078, the engagement endpoints serve from the meta DB while other endpoints serve from the active engagement's DB — this two-database API server is documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10). The MCP server has the same lifecycle. UX cost: a few seconds of API unavailability per switch with a 'Switching engagement...' indicator in the desktop. External clients during the switch see connection-refused or timeout. PI-017 is authored to track the multi-tenant migration with the prototype-to-production trigger. CBM Phase 1 can run on v0.5 (one-process-per-engagement) without waiting for PI-017.

decisions.title: Identifier scope: per-engagement for governance and methodology identifiers; engagement identifiers scoped to meta DB
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q5. With per-engagement DBs (DEC-078, DEC-079), the question is whether governance identifiers (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, etc.) and methodology identifiers (DOM-NNN, ENT-NNN, PROC-NNN, CRMC-NNN) sequence globally across all engagements or per-engagement. CBM's first session being SES-025 because the dogfood happens to have 24 prior sessions would be absurd; per-engagement scope is the only sane choice for review/auditability. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers (from DEC-068 slice A) query the active DB connection — so per-engagement scope happens automatically without v0.5 helper changes. The remaining question is mostly about confirming the obvious answer.
decisions.decision: Governance and methodology identifiers (SES, DEC, PI, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC) scope per-engagement: each engagement's DB has its own identifier sequences. CBM's first session is SES-001 within the CBM engagement, independent of the dogfood's SES-025+. Engagement identifiers (ENG-NNN) scope to the meta DB: one sequence per v2 install. On Doug's laptop, the dogfood is ENG-001, CBM is ENG-002, etc. Reinstalling v2 from scratch and recreating engagements resets the ENG sequence; engagement_code values remain the stable human-facing handle.
decisions.rationale: Per-engagement scope for governance/methodology is the only sane choice. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers query the active DB connection, so per-engagement scope is automatic with no v0.5 changes to the helpers themselves. Engagement identifiers naturally scope to the meta DB because that is where engagement records live. Identifier collisions across engagements don't matter: engagement A's DEC-001 and engagement B's DEC-001 are distinct decisions in distinct engagements, related only by external context. The meta DB has its own next-identifier helper (GET /engagements/next-identifier) following the v0.4 pattern.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Global identifier scope across all engagements (CBM's first session is SES-025+). Rejected as obviously wrong on review/auditability grounds. Engagement identifiers scoped per-engagement (so the dogfood and CBM both have ENG-001). Rejected because engagement records live in the meta DB and there is no per-engagement scope to apply at this level — the meta DB is the single sequence's home.
decisions.consequences: No changes to the v0.4 next-identifier helpers themselves; they continue to query the active DB connection and naturally return per-engagement sequences. A new next-identifier helper GET /engagements/next-identifier is added to the v0.5 build, served from the meta DB. CBM's first session record (whenever CBM Phase 1 runs) is SES-001. The dogfood engagement's sessions continue at SES-026+. Identifier collisions across engagements are accepted as not-actually-collisions: they refer to distinct records in distinct engagement scopes.

decisions.title: Migrations across engagements: lazy at engagement-open via run_engagement_migrations helper
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q6. When a new Alembic migration ships (e.g., v0.6 adds a column to the domain table for domain_short_code per PI-007), each engagement's DB needs the migration applied. Three viable options: (A) lazy at engagement-open — apply pending migrations when the engagement is activated; (B) eager at v2 launch — apply pending migrations to all engagements at desktop startup; (C) explicit only — never automatic, user runs a CLI command. Affects operational UX after every release that ships a migration. v1 uses Option A (run_client_migrations runs at activation per v1's ActiveClientContext).
decisions.decision: Lazy at engagement-open. When an engagement is activated, the desktop launcher runs alembic upgrade head against that engagement's DB before spawning the API subprocess. Mirrors v1's run_client_migrations pattern. Each engagement's alembic_version table tracks its own head; no cross-engagement coupling. Engagements that are never opened never get migrated; engagements that are opened after a long pause apply pending migrations at activation. The meta DB has its own Alembic chain that applies at desktop launch (before any engagement is listable) — failure to migrate the meta DB is a hard-fail UX.
decisions.rationale: Matches v1's proven pattern. Defers work to when it is actually needed; never-opened engagements pay no cost. Failure modes are scoped to one engagement at a time — a broken migration affects activation of one engagement, not the entire v2 install or all engagements at startup. Eager-at-launch (Option B) creates correlated failure risk (every engagement fails if the migration has a bug, blocking the desktop from launching) and pays cost for engagements the user isn't using. Explicit-only (Option C) creates friction for normal use without a clear win — Alembic migrations applied at known checkpoints (release boundaries) are well-understood, and the engine should 'just work' when the codebase has moved forward.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — eager at v2 launch. Rejected on correlated-failure-risk grounds and on cost-for-engagements-not-used grounds. Option C — explicit only. Rejected because it creates friction for normal use without a clear safety win; well-tested Alembic migrations applied lazily at activation are the standard pattern.
decisions.consequences: v0.5 build adds a helper (call it run_engagement_migrations) that takes an engagement DB path and applies pending migrations. The desktop's activation sequence calls this helper before spawning the API subprocess (Step 3 in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4's 12-step sequence). The activation UX shows an 'Upgrading engagement database...' indicator when migrations are running; for an engagement already at head, the helper is nearly free. Forward-only migrations: same posture as v0.4. The meta DB's own Alembic chain applies at desktop launch — meta DB cannot be opened if its migrations have failed. Acceptance criterion: 'open a stale engagement DB with a newer codebase; activation applies pending migrations; engagement opens with schema at head.'

decisions.title: Dogfood migration: one-shot explicit migration moves v2.db to engagements/CRMBUILDER.db at v0.5 first launch
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q7. The existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db file (which v0.4 ships with) holds the v2-build's own governance data: 24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, 66 references, 222 change_log entries, 42 catalog entries, 13 status versions, 2 charter versions, plus topics and risks. v0.5 needs to retrofit this into the new engagement model without losing or corrupting any of it. Three options: (A) one-shot explicit migration with backup-first / verify-after / then-delete discipline; (B) dual-path compatibility (engine knows both old and new paths for the dogfood forever); (C) default-engagement special case (the dogfood's path is allowed to live outside engagements/ via a configurable engagement_db_path field). Coupled to DEC-079 (Q2 chose convention; B and C compromise that retroactively).
decisions.decision: One-shot explicit migration runs at v0.5 first launch when the engine detects an existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db and a missing or empty meta DB. The migration is idempotent on rerun. Operations: (1) Backup v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db.pre-v0.5-backup (left in place; user can delete manually after confirming v0.5 works). (2) Create the meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db and run its initial Alembic migration to head. (3) INSERT the CRMBUILDER engagement row with the v0.5 migration's specified field values (engagement_identifier=ENG-001, engagement_code=CRMBUILDER, engagement_name='CRM Builder v2', engagement_purpose='Dogfood instance hosting the v2 build's own governance content (sessions, decisions, planning items, methodology catalog).', engagement_status=active, engagement_export_dir=absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ computed from engine repo root, audit timestamps set to migration time). (4) Copy v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. (5) Open the new path; query expected row counts for sessions, decisions, planning_items, refs, change_log, charter, status, base_entity_catalog, topics, risks; each count must match the source. (6) Delete the original v2.db (the .pre-v0.5-backup copy remains). (7) Refresh the JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ from the new path. Three install scenarios handled: existing v2 install (migrate), fresh install with no v2.db (no-op; meta DB created empty; first-launch UX guides creating engagements explicitly), rerun after successful migration (detect already-migrated state and exit cleanly).
decisions.rationale: Q2 chose convention (DEC-079); Q7 respects Q2's choice rather than carve out a permanent exception. The dogfood deserves the same convention every future engagement gets — both for consistency and because every line of 'is this the dogfood path or the new path?' logic is debt the codebase pays forever. The migration is a one-time cost; the special-case paths in Options B and C are a forever cost. Backup-first / verify-row-counts / then-delete discipline is well-understood standard discipline for destructive migrations, not novel risk.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — dual-path compatibility (engine reads from both v2.db and engagements/CRMBUILDER.db forever). Rejected because it accrues permanent technical debt in every code path that opens the dogfood, defeats the Q2 convention, and creates a forever-special-case for the dogfood that the workstream plan explicitly does not want ('the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several'). Option C — default-engagement special case with engagement_db_path field on the engagement record pointing at the old path. Rejected because it would require reopening Q2's no-path-field decision and would compromise the Q2 convention with a permanent exception just for the dogfood.
decisions.consequences: v0.5 build includes a self-contained migration module invoked by the launcher at first-launch detection of the migration-needed state. Slice placement (Slice A foundation versus a dedicated migration slice) is explicitly a Conversation 2 question per the workstream plan §10. Failure recovery: the .pre-v0.5-backup file is the recovery point; the user is told to revert by deleting the new files and reverting code to the prior v2 release. All eight tracked tables plus topics and risks have row counts in the new path matching the source. Idempotency: rerunning after successful migration is a no-op. The dogfood engagement record (CRMBUILDER) is born at migration time with all the field values specified in this decision; engagement.md §3 details the schema and the engagement record values are documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.7 and engagement.md.

decisions.title: Per-engagement exports: split via nullable engagement_export_dir field on the meta DB engagement record
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q8. v0.4's db-export JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ are the git-tracked render of the dogfood's content. For v0.5, the question is where each engagement's exports go. Three options: (A) split — dogfood exports stay in the engine repo; client engagement exports land in the client repo via a path field on the engagement record; (B) all in engine repo organized by code (reviewers would have to look in two places for a client engagement's documents); (C) manual command only for client engagements (creates a two-class engagement model). Coupled to Q2 (which chose convention — no path field for DBs) — Q8 accepts an asymmetry because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents.
decisions.decision: Exports for each engagement land where the engagement's other documents live. A nullable engagement_export_dir column is added to the meta DB engagements table (TEXT, validated when set: must be an existing writable directory; absolute filesystem path). Dogfood's value is set by the v0.5 dogfood migration (DEC-084) to the absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ (computed from the engine repo root). Client engagements set their value at creation time via the New-Engagement dialog (user browses to the client repo or types the path). Export-refresh writes to the active engagement's engagement_export_dir; null disables auto-export with a log warning at activation. The existing v0.4 export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's engagement_export_dir.
decisions.rationale: Each engagement's git-tracked lineage belongs with the engagement's other documents, where reviewers will naturally look. For the dogfood, that's the engine repo (where the v2-build's PRDs and methodology documents live); for client engagements (CBM, future clients), that's the client repo (where the engagement's process docs, entity PRDs, etc. live). Forcing all exports into a centralised engine-repo location would split each client engagement's documentation between two repos. The asymmetry with Q2 (DB files in engine repo) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data and exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B — all in engine repo organized by code (db-export/engagements/{code}/). Rejected because reviewers of a CBM engagement's lineage would have to look in two repos (CBM for PRDs and process docs, engine repo for JSON snapshots), defeating the natural-home principle. Option C — manual command only for client engagements (no auto-export for client engagements; dogfood-only auto-export). Rejected because it creates a two-class engagement model where the dogfood is special, contradicting the workstream plan's framing that the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several.
decisions.consequences: engagement_export_dir nullable TEXT column added to the meta DB engagements table. The dogfood migration sets this field for the CRMBUILDER row to the computed absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. The New-Engagement dialog (built in v0.5 Slice B) includes an engagement_export_dir field (optional; user can leave blank and set later via Edit). v0.4's export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's value. Drift handling: if the path is set but doesn't exist or isn't writable, the export attempt logs a warning and skips; user fixes via Edit Engagement. Null values produce a log warning at activation and no file writes. Absolute paths in the meta DB are fine because the meta DB is gitignored data; machine moves require fresh install and re-creation of engagements, which re-sets paths.

decisions.title: Engagement entity schema, identifier, status lifecycle, and API surface
decisions.context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q10 and Q10b. The engagement entity type is the sole new methodology entity in v0.5. Q10 asks for the field set; Q10b asks for the status lifecycle. The kickoff's weak prior for fields (engagement_identifier, engagement_code mirroring v1, engagement_name, engagement_purpose, engagement_status, audit timestamps, possibly engagement_data_dir if Q2 settles on configurable paths) plus three lifecycle options (domain pattern, active/paused/archived, active/archived). Coupled to all prior decisions in this conversation: DEC-078 (engagement record lives in meta DB), DEC-079 (no engagement_db_path), DEC-080 (engagement_last_opened_at column), DEC-082 (ENG identifier scope), DEC-085 (engagement_export_dir column).
decisions.decision: The engagement entity type has these properties. Identifier prefix: ENG (3 letters; no collision with existing prefixes; format ENG-NNN zero-padded to 3 digits; server-assigned on POST omission; helper at GET /engagements/next-identifier). Storage: meta DB engagements table at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. Fields (all prefixed engagement_ per DEC-046): engagement_identifier (TEXT, required, server-assigned, ^ENG-\d{3}$, unique), engagement_code (TEXT, required, regex ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$ mirroring v1's Client.code exactly, case-insensitive unique within the meta DB), engagement_name (TEXT, required, case-insensitive unique), engagement_purpose (TEXT, required, plain text 1-2 sentences), engagement_status (TEXT, required, default 'active', enum {active, paused, archived}, free transitions), engagement_last_opened_at (DATETIME, nullable, per DEC-080), engagement_export_dir (TEXT, nullable absolute path validated when set, per DEC-085), engagement_created_at / engagement_updated_at / engagement_deleted_at (inherited base behavior). Three deliberate omissions from the domain.md pattern: no engagement_description (purpose suffices), no engagement_notes (engagement is routing metadata not methodology content; scratchpad notes belong inside the per-engagement DB), no engagement_db_path (DEC-079 chose convention). Status lifecycle: active/paused/archived with free transitions, default starter 'active'. Engagement records are direct creations (not propose-verified by a client), so domain's one-way candidate→confirmed gate doesn't fit. Three states capture real operational lifecycle (active work, on hold, wound up); free transitions match reality (engagements can resume from any state). Soft-delete independent of status. No relationships in v0.5. API surface: standard endpoint set (list, get, create, full replace, partial update, soft-delete, restore, next-identifier) served from the meta DB. No activate endpoint — activation is desktop-side orchestration per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4. UI shape: deviates from the spec guide's default Methodology-sidebar-group placement because engagement is structurally different (v2-install-level routing metadata, not domain-scope content); two needed affordances (switching UI and management UI); exact visual layout deferred to Conversation 2 in coordination with the styling workstream (PI-001) per DEC-076's boundary discipline. Full spec at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md with 12 acceptance criteria in §3.7.
decisions.rationale: Field set follows DEC-046 (parent-prefix naming) and the established methodology-entity-schema-design conventions, adapted for engagement's structural difference. engagement_code mirroring v1's Client.code exactly (^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$, length 2-10) lets v1 and v2 co-reference the same engagement by code if useful later. The three omissions (description, notes, db_path) are justified by engagement's nature as routing metadata rather than methodology content. Lifecycle: engagement records are direct creations not propose-verified, so domain's one-way gate doesn't fit (Option B). Three states capture the real operational lifecycle without ceremony; two states (Option C) drops 'paused' which has real-world meaning. Free transitions match reality (an engagement on hold can resume; an archived engagement can be reactivated if a client comes back). UI deviation justified by engagement's structural difference from the four v0.4 methodology entities; the Methodology sidebar group is for domain-scope content, not for v2-install-level routing metadata, and engagement needs two distinct affordances (always-visible switcher and occasional-access manager) rather than one master/detail panel.
decisions.alternatives_considered: For status lifecycle: Option B (domain pattern candidate/confirmed/deferred with one-way gate). Rejected because engagement records aren't proposed-then-verified; adopting the domain pattern would create awkward UX ('why is my engagement in candidate state? Who verifies it?'). Option C (active/archived, two states). Rejected because it drops the 'temporarily on hold' state which has real operational meaning; users would either keep paused engagements 'active' and remember they're on hold (fragile) or repeatedly archive/unarchive (friction). For the field set: adding engagement_description, engagement_notes, or engagement_db_path was considered for each. Rejected case-by-case as described in the decision text. For the UI shape: a ListDetailPanel in a new 'Engagements' sidebar group was a default candidate; the conversation surfaced that engagement is structurally different and deferred the exact visual layout to Conversation 2 with explicit deviation rationale.
decisions.consequences: The meta DB's engagements table has the ten columns specified. The Alembic migration for the meta DB creates this table at v0.5 first launch (or via the dogfood migration's step 2 per DEC-084). v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements: the access-layer methods (list_engagements, get_engagement, create_engagement, update_engagement, patch_engagement, delete_engagement, restore_engagement, next_engagement_identifier), the eight REST endpoints, the meta-DB connection handling in the API server (the two-database server detail per multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10), and the engagement UI (switching and management affordances; exact layout from Conversation 2). The dogfood migration sets engagement_status=active for the CRMBUILDER row. New engagements created via the New-Engagement dialog default to active. Free transitions between active/paused/archived are accepted; transitions to values outside the enum return 422 invalid_enum_value. Soft-delete via DELETE sets engagement_deleted_at without touching the per-engagement DB file; restore via POST /restore clears it. Acceptance criteria: 12 testable statements in engagement.md §3.7 covering schema migration, identifier/code constraint enforcement, status enum and transition validation, export_dir validation, access-layer methods, REST endpoints, soft-delete/restore round-trip, meta-DB serving, engagement_last_opened_at updates on activation, and the dogfood/CBM sample-record scenario.

planning_items.title: Migrate API and MCP servers to multi-tenant model
planning_items.description:
Anchored by DEC-081. v0.5 ships one API and MCP subprocess per engagement (Option A); multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture for v2's production phase. The migration lands when v2 transitions from prototype (v0.x releases) to production (v1.0). Trigger is a planned transition, not a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern of friction triggers that never fire).

Scope of the migration when it lands:

1. Refactor every REST endpoint to accept engagement context. Two viable mechanisms: a request header (X-Engagement: ENG-NNN) or a path-prefix (/v1/engagements/ENG-NNN/sessions, etc.). The mechanism is settled when the workstream opens; both options have precedent in similar systems.

2. Refactor the access-layer connection model to hold per-engagement connections. Options include: a connection pool keyed by engagement_identifier opened lazily on first use; eager pool warming at startup; or per-request connection acquire/release with engagement-context binding. The pool strategy is settled by the workstream.

3. Refactor tests for engagement-context fixtures. Every test that today asserts behavior against a single DB needs to express which engagement it operates against. v0.4's test suite is the regression net.

4. Reshape the MCP server to mirror the multi-tenant API. MCP tools either gain an engagement-context input argument or a per-tool engagement selector. Specific shape is settled by the workstream.

5. Remove the kill-relaunch dance from the desktop's engagement-switch path. The desktop's ActiveEngagementContext continues to exist but switching becomes a fast in-process operation rather than a process-lifecycle dance. The 'Switching engagement...' UX indicator becomes vestigial or is removed.

6. Consider adding an activate API endpoint (POST /engagements/{identifier}/activate) at this time. The endpoint becomes possible once the API server can switch engagements without restarting; whether to expose it depends on the workstream's posture on cross-engagement scripting use cases.

Dependencies and ordering: PI-017's workstream is sequenced after v0.5 ships and after enough real-use signal has accumulated (CBM Phase 1 underway or completed; possibly additional client engagements in flight) to validate that the prototype-to-production transition is the right moment. The exact timing is determined at the production-readiness workstream's opening kickoff conversation, not pre-committed here.

Note: this PI is not the same as the deferred 'cross-engagement reporting' or 'engagement-level access control' workstreams. Those are separate v0.6+ candidates. PI-017 is strictly the infrastructure migration; cross-engagement features become technically feasible after PI-017 ships but are out of PI-017's scope.
